title: Text Inputs & Select
intro: `wf-input` and `wf-select` are the two text-entry primitives of the wireframe kit. Both lean on a shared `wf-field` wrapper for their label, helper, and error scaffolding, so a field's structure stays identical whether it holds a free-text input or a dropdown.

blocks.body:
Reach for `wf-input` for any single-line text entry and `wf-select` for a fixed set of choices. Neither is meant to stand alone — wrap each in a `wf-field` div so the label, helper text, and error message stack in the right order with the right spacing.

`wf-input` is a bare `<input>` that fills its container (`width: 100%`) and accepts any HTML input `type` — `text`, `email`, `password`, `number`, `date`, `search` all inherit the same look. `wf-select` is a native `<select>` with its OS chevron suppressed (`appearance: none`) and replaced by an inline SVG caret, plus `36px` of right padding so option text never collides with it.

State is expressed three ways: native attributes (`disabled`), native pseudo-classes (`:focus`, `:hover`), and one wrapper modifier — `wf-field-error` on the parent `wf-field` — which restyles the control and is the only state that is a class rather than an attribute.

blocks.title: Input — Basic with Label
blocks.caption: `wf-input` text fields in their default rest state, each paired with a `wf-field-label` — shown with a placeholder and without.

blocks.title: wf-input states
blocks.intro: There is one base class — `wf-input` — and state comes from native attributes, native pseudo-classes, or the parent `wf-field-error` modifier. The wrapper structure never changes.

rows.name: wf-input
rows.when: The resting default. White fill, 1px #cbd5e1 border, 6px radius. Always inside a wf-field.

rows.name: wf-field-helper
rows.when: Supporting guidance beneath the field — 11px muted grey, lives after the input inside the same wf-field.
rows.code: We'll never share your email

rows.name: :focus
rows.when: Automatic on keyboard or click focus. Border turns to --wf-color-border-focus (#2563eb) with a 3px focus ring; never style this manually.
rows.code:
<!-- native :focus — no extra class needed -->
<input type="text" class="wf-input">

rows.name: :disabled
rows.when: Set the native disabled attribute. Fill greys to #f1f5f9, text to #94a3b8, cursor becomes not-allowed.

rows.name: wf-field-error (on parent)
rows.when: Add to the wf-field wrapper to flag a validation error. Repaints the input red-bordered with a pink fill and pairs with wf-field-error-message.
rows.code: Username is already taken

blocks.title: Input — Error State
blocks.caption: The `wf-field-error` modifier on the wrapper: red border (#dc2626), pink fill (#fef2f2), and a `wf-field-error-message` line beneath the field.

blocks.title: Input — Disabled State
blocks.caption: Disabled `wf-input` fields with a greyed value (#94a3b8) on a #f1f5f9 fill, optionally followed by `wf-field-helper` text.

blocks.title: Select — Basic Dropdown
blocks.caption: `wf-select` dropdowns in both the placeholder/unselected state and a pre-selected value state, each with the custom SVG chevron affordance.

blocks.title: wf-select states
blocks.intro: `wf-select` mirrors `wf-input` exactly — same border, radius, focus ring, disabled treatment, and the same `wf-field-error` wrapper modifier. It adds a native-chevron suppression and a custom caret.

rows.name: wf-select
rows.when: The resting default. Use an empty-value first option as the placeholder prompt.

rows.name: selected option
rows.when: Mark a default-selected choice with the native selected attribute on its option.

rows.name: optgroup
rows.when: Group long option lists with native optgroup — the chevron and padding accommodate them unchanged.
rows.code:
<select class="wf-select">
  <option value="">Select a terminal...</option>
  <optgroup label="East Coast">
    <option value="nyc">New York</option>
    <option value="bos">Boston</option>
  </optgroup>
</select>

rows.name: :disabled
rows.when: Native disabled attribute — same #f1f5f9 fill, #94a3b8 text, not-allowed cursor as wf-input.

rows.name: wf-field-error (on parent)
rows.when: Add to the wf-field wrapper to flag a missing/invalid selection; pairs with wf-field-error-message.
rows.code: Please select a region

blocks.title: Tokens these controls consume
blocks.intro: Both controls are built entirely from `--wf-*` variables — restyle by overriding the token, not the control class.

rows.use: Resting 1px border on both wf-input and wf-select.
Border on hover, only when not disabled and not focused.
Border color on :focus for both controls.
Border color applied by the wf-field-error wrapper modifier.
Pink fill applied to the control inside wf-field-error.
Fill of a disabled input or select.
Text color of a disabled control.
Focus ring on a healthy control.
Focus ring when the field is in error.
Corner radius shared by both controls.

blocks.title: Canonical field markup
blocks.code:
We'll never share your email
Please enter a valid email address
Required for inventory classification
blocks.note: Order inside wf-field is fixed: label first, control second, then exactly one of wf-field-helper or wf-field-error-message.

items.rule: Always wrap a control in wf-field.
items.why: wf-field supplies the 4px label-to-control gap and the 16px bottom margin; a bare wf-input has no label slot and collides with its neighbors.

items.rule: Error state lives on the wrapper, not the control.
items.why: The repaint is keyed off `.wf-field-error .wf-input` / `.wf-field-error .wf-select` — adding a class to the input itself does nothing.

items.rule: Use a wf-field-error-message in error, a wf-field-helper otherwise — not both.
items.why: Stacking both doubles the gap and competes for the user's eye; the error message replaces the helper while the field is invalid.

items.rule: Mark required fields with a wf-required asterisk in the label.
items.why: It's the only required-state affordance the kit ships; the native required attribute alone is invisible until submit.
Both controls already swap to the #2563eb border plus a 3px ring — a custom outline fights the token-driven one and breaks the error-focus ring.
The error styles target the control as a descendant of .wf-field-error — putting the modifier on the input itself selects nothing and the field stays neutral.

items.dont: Turn the field red with no message
items.why: The red border and pink fill say 'something is wrong' but not what — wf-field-error-message is the only place the reason gets stated.

items.do: Swap the helper out for the error message in the error state
items.dont: Keep wf-field-helper and add wf-field-error-message below it
items.why: Two stacked lines of micro-copy crowd the field; the error supersedes the helper until the value is valid again.

items.do: Let :focus paint the red focus ring while in error
items.dont: Override the focus box-shadow on errored fields
items.why: wf-field-error scopes its own --wf-shadow-focus-error ring; a manual override loses the red tint and the field looks healthy on focus.

items.title: wf-select hides the native chevron
items.detail: appearance: none (with -webkit- and -moz- prefixes) suppresses the OS caret and an inline SVG polyline draws the replacement at right 10px center. The 36px of right padding reserves room for it — don't shrink the padding or option text overlaps the caret.

items.title: Hover is suppressed while focused or disabled
items.detail: Both hover rules are scoped :hover:not(:disabled):not(:focus). A focused or disabled control deliberately won't pick up the #94a3b8 hover border — that's intended, not a bug.

items.title: wf-input fills its container
items.detail: width: 100% with box-sizing: border-box means the field is exactly as wide as its parent. Control width by sizing the wf-field (or its container), not the input.

items.title: Placeholder and helper share one muted grey
items.detail: Both --wf-color-text-muted (#64748b). An empty input with placeholder text and its helper line read at the same weight — rely on position (inside vs. below) to tell them apart.
